jonnify·bcs /bcs/cache/single-writer-ring
Why The proof Dives References

B4.3 · The Single Writer and the Ring — manuscript chapter 4.3

The single writer applies the stream.

Chapter 4.2 gated coherence's semantics; this chapter changes nothing about them and everything about the engine underneath. Application moves out of the table's owner and onto a bounded ring drained by one applier — the part's fifth law as a data structure. One production module lands (EchoCache.Ring: two atomic sequences, preallocated ETS slots, edge-triggered wakes, batched drains, occupancy as a gauge, drop-on-full as a counted refusal), and the committed record carries the shape in numbers: 1000 items in publish order through 2 batches (largest 801) on 1 wakes; 1005116 items per second on one scheduler; a 500-invalidation storm applied in 25 ms while a fill fired mid-storm completed in 0 ms; and 500 shuffled messages converging on exactly 100 applied and 400 stale.

The chapter is content/bcs4.3.md; the rung behind it is bcs_rung_4_3_check.exs, and its committed transcript closes PASS 6/6 under the header Valkey on 6390 | Elixir 1.14.0 OTP 25 | schedulers 1. Six gates, each derived before it is measured.

§1

Why the owner stops applying

content/bcs4.3.md · Why · Decisions

In 4.2 the table's owner did two jobs: it served fills and puts, and it applied every coherence push inline. At 72 microseconds a message that coupling is invisible — until the storm. A halted market segment, a bulk reprice, a reconnect replay: thousands of invalidations arrive in one burst, and an owner that applies inline queues its fills behind them, while an owner that spawns per message buys unbounded process churn for work that is one ETS comparison each. The mature answer has a name and a pedigree: LMAX built a retail exchange whose business logic ran on a single thread — "6 million orders per second on a single thread" — fed by the Disruptor, a bounded ring of preallocated slots with sequence counters. This chapter translates that shape onto the BEAM with the primitives the runtime ships for it, and reads it beside the bus's own park-don't-poll: both replace discovery with arrival, and both wake exactly once per busy period.

BROADCAST ONLY

The ring serves the broadcast lane only

Drop-on-full preserves at-most-once; "routing the job lane's at-least-once delivery through a dropping structure would launder a guarantee away." Two lanes, two engines, one comparison making their races safe.

DROP, COUNTED

Drop, never block, never overwrite

Blocking turns a full ring into owner backpressure and mailbox growth; overwriting loses a message silently and unpredictably. A counted refusal keeps both the lane's contract and the operator's visibility.

ONE PRODUCER

Single producer, structurally

The publish path is lock-free because exactly one process calls it — the owner, where pushes already serialize. The simplicity is bought with a rule, and the rule is stated in the module doc.

CALLER-SIDE

The applier applies caller-side

apply_batch/2 touches public ETS and the spec's counters, never the owner — the whole point of the decoupling — and every interleaving with concurrent fills converges because newer-wins is a comparison.

PERSISTENT_TERM

Runtime in persistent_term

One persistent_term read per publish — the BEAM's cheapest shared-read path, written once at ring start and erased at stop. A brutal kill leaks one entry until the name is reused — "a stated cost of kill-9 truthfulness."

THE GAUGE

Occupancy is the gauge

Tail minus head, readable by anyone at any time. A dashboard that plots this number shows a storm as a hill instead of inferring it from tail latencies.

§2

The proof

content/bcs4.3.md · quoting bcs_rung_4_3_check.out

The full committed record, verbatim — the header, six gates, and four derive lines. The derive lines are part of the record: each measurement is bounded before it is taken, and the committed line then lands inside its own band.

The derivation note rides with the record (D-B4.3): "the first band's ceiling undercounted exactly because it priced the apply side that batching had already amortized away." A storm-rated table is declared, not assembled — verbatim from the chapter's How:

{:ok, _} =
  EchoCache.Table.start_link(
    name: :quotes,
    kind: "AST",
    coherence: :broadcast,
    ring_capacity: 4_096,
    ttl_ms: 5_000,
    loader: &PriceFeed.load/1,
    connector: [port: 6390]
  )

The ring lives at runtimes/elixir/lib/echo_cache/ring.ex; the table's growth at lib/echo_cache/table.ex — apply_batch/2 running caller-side against public ETS and the spec's counters, the :broadcast init starting the ring before the subscription that feeds it, the push handler reduced to parse-and-publish, the spec carrying ring and ring_capacity, the terminate stopping the ring with the table.

§3

The dives

three lessons, fixed by the chapter spec
B4.3.1

Two Sequences, One Table

G1 — the surface and the truthful declaration: capacity 512 in the directory, a fresh ring at occupancy 0. G2 — order through batches: 1000 items crossed the ring in publish order exactly … through 2 batches (largest 801) on 1 wakes; the atomics ordering sentence; the Disruptor correspondence.

dive 1built B4.3.2

Occupancy and the Bound

G3 — the gauge: mid-storm the gauge read 600 of 4096 and drained to exactly 0, 1005116 items per second on one scheduler. G4 — full as a counted refusal: 64 accepted, 136 refused with :dropped and counted -- never blocked, never overwritten.

dive 2built B4.3.3

The Storm Drill

G5 — 500 invalidations crossed the wire and the ring in 25 ms with nothing dropped, a fill fired mid-storm completed in 0 ms. G6 — convergence: exactly on 100 applied and 400 stale -- arrival order changed nothing.

dive 3built

The module hands forward: B4.4 · The Lane That Remembers — the journal writer is the next single-writer candidate; one owner draining ordered work is about to become a habit. And the manuscript plans the referee chapter — B4.5 · The Cache Referee — to hold the comparison set to this module's drills.

§4

References

sources · related

Sources

  • Fowler — The LMAX Architecturethe single-threaded business logic processor and the ring that feeds it: the prior art this chapter translates
  • LMAX Disruptor — technical papersequences over preallocated slots; batching as the consumer's catch-up effect
  • Erlang/OTP — atomicsthe sequences' visibility guarantee: "all atomic operations are mutually ordered"

Related

  • B4 — EchoCachethe chapter landing; Part IV's arc

  • B4.1 — Cache-Aside at ETS Speedthe owner returned to its job description

  • B2.2 — Property Storesthe stores behind the cache
  • Redis Patterns Appliedthe caching substrate patterns
  • EchoMQ — the coursethe bus protocol in rung-level depth
